source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 6.6.5-6.7 file operations, config, visual mode (pp. 95-97)

questions.q: Launch the built-in interactive tutorial for learning VI.
questions.why: vimtutor opens a guided lesson that teaches VI/vim hands-on.

questions.q: Insert (paste) the contents of the file notes.txt after the cursor.
questions.why: :r file reads another file's contents into the buffer after the cursor.

questions.q: Discard your unsaved changes and reload the last saved version of the file.
questions.why: :e! reloads the file from its last saved state, throwing away the current buffer's changes.

questions.q: Save lines 5 through 10 into a separate file called part.txt.
questions.why: :n,m w file writes just the given line range (here 5 to 10) to another file.

questions.q: Open /home/rockstar/file and turn on line numbering at startup, using the -c option.
questions.why: -c runs an ex command as VI opens; here 'set nu' enables line numbers for the session.

questions.q: How can you make ex commands run automatically every time VI/vim starts?
options: Put them in a file named .exrc in your login (home) directory
Pass them on every command line with -x
There is no way to persist them
questions.why: Ex commands placed in ~/.exrc are read and applied at each VI/VIM startup.

questions.q: In visual mode, what is the difference between v and V?
options: v selects at the character level; V selects at the line level
v selects lines; V selects characters
v enters insert mode; V enters ex mode
questions.why: Lowercase v marks a character-level selection, uppercase V marks whole lines; visual mode is a sub-mode of command mode.

questions.q: Having marked a selection in visual mode (with v or V), which keys delete it and which copy it?
options: x deletes (cuts) the marked selection; y copies (yanks) it
questions.why: After marking with the arrow keys, x cuts the highlighted text and y copies it.
